/*  Self-contained SWI-Prolog program
    Jurisdiction: South Carolina
    Focus year: 2021

    Question:
    "Can a landlord evict a tenant for material falsification on the rental application?"
*/

% -----------------------------
% Scenario facts (question context)
% -----------------------------

state(south_carolina).
focus_year(2021).

tenant(t1).
landlord(l1).

rental_agreement(ra1).
agreement_between(ra1, l1, t1).

% The issue alleged by the landlord
breach_type(material_falsification_on_rental_application).
breach_of(ra1, t1, material_falsification_on_rental_application).

% Helper predicate required by the prompt: succeeds when the scenario is set up.
scenario_statement :-
    state(south_carolina),
    focus_year(2021),
    tenant(t1),
    landlord(l1),
    rental_agreement(ra1),
    agreement_between(ra1, l1, t1),
    breach_of(ra1, t1, material_falsification_on_rental_application).

% -----------------------------
% Statute translations (deterministic predicates)
% -----------------------------

/*
  [1] S.C. CODE § 27-37-10(A)(1)-(3)
  Tenant may be ejected upon landlord application when:
   (1) fails/refuses to pay rent when due/demanded,
   (2) term ended,
   (3) terms/conditions of lease violated.
*/
statute_sc_27_37_10_ground(failure_to_pay_rent).
statute_sc_27_37_10_ground(term_ended).
statute_sc_27_37_10_ground(lease_violation).

/*
  [2] S.C. CODE § 27-40-710(A)
  For noncompliance other than nonpayment of rent, or certain health/safety
  code violations, landlord may deliver a 14-day notice; if not remedied,
  rental agreement terminates; landlord may then bring action for possession.
*/
statute_sc_27_40_710_requires_notice(nonpayment_of_rent, no).
statute_sc_27_40_710_requires_notice(building_code_noncompliance_health_safety, no).
statute_sc_27_40_710_requires_notice(other_noncompliance, yes).

statute_sc_27_40_710_notice_days(other_noncompliance, 14).

/*
  [3] S.C. CODE § 27-40-770(A)-(B)
  Termination periods for week-to-week and month-to-month tenancies.
  Not essential to the question here, but included as translated statutes.
*/
statute_sc_27_40_770_termination_days(week_to_week, 7).
statute_sc_27_40_770_termination_days(month_to_month, 30).

/*
  [4] S.C. CODE § 27-40-770(C)
  After expiration/termination, landlord may bring action for possession.
  Additional remedies for holdover.
*/
statute_sc_27_40_770_allows_action_for_possession(holdover_after_expiration_or_termination).

/*
  [5] S.C. CODE § 27-40-910(C)(1)-(3)
  Landlord may bring action for possession if:
   (1) code violation caused primarily by tenant/lack of reasonable care,
   (2) material noncompliance under § 27-40-710 or § 27-40-720,
   (3) code compliance requires alteration/remodeling/demolition depriving tenant use.
*/
statute_sc_27_40_910_c_ground(code_violation_caused_by_tenant).
statute_sc_27_40_910_c_ground(material_noncompliance_under_27_40_710_or_720).
statute_sc_27_40_910_c_ground(code_compliance_requires_alteration_depriving_use).

/*
  [6] S.C. CODE § 27-40-510(1)-(7)
  Tenant duties (selected ones relevant to "material noncompliance").
*/
statute_sc_27_40_510_duty(comply_with_building_and_housing_codes_health_safety).
statute_sc_27_40_510_duty(keep_dwelling_reasonably_safe_and_clean).
statute_sc_27_40_510_duty(dispose_waste_reasonably).
statute_sc_27_40_510_duty(keep_plumbing_fixtures_clean).
statute_sc_27_40_510_duty(use_facilities_reasonably).
statute_sc_27_40_510_duty(not_destroy_deface_damage_remove_premises).
statute_sc_27_40_510_duty(not_disturb_peaceful_enjoyment).

/*
  [7] S.C. CODE § 27-40-540
  Tenant shall not conduct illegal activities in the dwelling unit.
*/
statute_sc_27_40_540_prohibits(illegal_activities_in_dwelling_unit).

% -----------------------------
% Mapping breach types to statutory grounds
% -----------------------------

% Map a breach type to a statutory ground that supports eviction.
% This is the key reasoning step for this question.
%
% NOTE: The provided statutes do not create a ground for eviction
%       based solely on "material falsification on the rental application".
%       Therefore, we intentionally provide no mapping for that breach type.
statute_ground_for_breach(_BreachType, _Ground) :-
    fail.

% -----------------------------

3. No judgment of possession shall be entered for any premises covered by section 2 of this act, except in the nonpayment of rent under subsection a. or f. of section 2, unless the landlord has made written demand and given written notice for delivery of possession of the premises. The following notice shall be required: a. For an action alleging disorderly conduct under subsection b. of section 2, or injury to the premises under subsection c. of section 2, or any grounds under subsection m., n., o., p., q., or r. of section 2, three days' notice prior to the institution of the action for possession; b. For an action alleging continued violation of rules and regulations under subsection d. of section 2, or substantial breach of covenant under subsection e. of section 2, or habitual failure to pay rent, one month's notice prior to the institution of the action for possession; c. For an action alleging any grounds under subsection g. of section 2, three months' notice prior to the institution of the action; d. For an action alleging permanent retirement under subsection h. of section 2, 18 months' notice prior to the institution of the action and, provided that, where there is a lease in effect, no action may be instituted until the lease expires; e. For an action alleging refusal of acceptance of reasonable lease changes under subsection i. of section 2, one month's notice prior to institution of action; f. For an action alleging any grounds under subsection l. of section 2, two months' notice prior to the institution of the action and, provided that where there is a written lease in effect no action shall be instituted until the lease expires; g. For an action alleging any grounds under subsection k. of section 2, three years' notice prior to the institution of action, and provided that where there is a written lease in effect, no action shall be instituted until the lease expires; h. In public housing under the control of a public housing authority or redevelopment agency, for an action alleging substantial breach of contract under paragraph (2) of subsection e. of section 2, the period of notice required prior to the institution of an action for possession shall be in accordance with federal regulations pertaining to public housing leases. The notice in each of the foregoing instances shall specify in detail the cause of the termination of the tenancy and shall be served either personally upon the tenant or lessee or such person in possession by giving him a copy thereof, or by leaving a copy thereof at his usual place of abode with some member of his family above the age of 14 years, or by certified mail; if the certified letter is not claimed, notice shall be sent by regular mail.

[2] N.J. STAT 2A:18-55

A late fee shall not be imposed in excess of the amount set forth in the application for a warrant for removal if all rent due and owing is paid within the three business day period established by this subsection. b. (1) A landlord shall accept all payments of rent made by a tenant within the three business day period established by subsection a. of this section and upon payment of the rent due and owing, within two business days thereafter, the landlord shall provide the court with written notice that the rent due and owing was paid. A copy of this notice shall be provided to the tenant. (2) Upon receipt of the written notice as provided in this subsection, the court shall dismiss with prejudice the action for nonpayment of rent. % HousingQA: New Jersey - Cure of Statutory Tenant Obligations

state(new_jersey).
focus_year(2021).

qa_item(
    new_jersey,
    "Must a landlord accept a tenant's attempt to cure for statutory tenant obligations?"
).

% Statutes
statute(nj_stat_2a_18_61_2).
statute(nj_stat_2a_18_55).
statute(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_f).
statute(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_e).
statute(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_d).
statute(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_b).
statute(nj_stat_2a_42_10_16a).

statute_of_state(nj_stat_2a_18_61_2, new_jersey).
statute_of_state(nj_stat_2a_18_55, new_jersey).
statute_of_state(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_f, new_jersey).
statute_of_state(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_e, new_jersey).
statute_of_state(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_d, new_jersey).
statute_of_state(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_b, new_jersey).
statute_of_state(nj_stat_2a_42_10_16a, new_jersey).

% Statutory facts

% [2] §2A:18-55 - Cure for nonpayment of rent (classic right to cure)
provides_cure(nj_stat_2a_18_55, nonpayment_of_rent).
requires_landlord_accept_cure(nj_stat_2a_18_55, nonpayment_of_rent).
cure_timeframe(nj_stat_2a_18_55, before_final_judgment).

% [7] §2A:42-10.16A - Cure after warrant for removal/lockout for nonpayment
provides_cure(nj_stat_2a_42_10_16a, nonpayment_of_rent_after_warrant).
requires_landlord_accept_cure(nj_stat_2a_42_10_16a, nonpayment_of_rent_after_warrant).
cure_timeframe(nj_stat_2a_42_10_16a, three_business_days_after_warrant_or_lockout).

% [4] §2A:18-61.1(e) - Breach of lease covenants (statutory obligations)
statutory_tenant_obligation(breach_of_lease_covenant).
cure_possible(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_e, breach_of_lease_covenant).
requires_notice_to_cease(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_e, breach_of_lease_covenant).

% [5] §2A:18-61.1(d) - Violation of rules and regulations (statutory obligations)
statutory_tenant_obligation(violation_of_rules).
cure_possible(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_d, violation_of_rules).
requires_notice_to_cease(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_d, violation_of_rules).

% [6] §2A:18-61.1(b) - Disorderly conduct (statutory obligations)
statutory_tenant_obligation(disorderly_conduct).
cure_possible(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_b, disorderly_conduct).
requires_notice_to_cease(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_b, disorderly_conduct).

% [3] §2A:18-61.1(f) - Failure to pay rent after notice of increase (statutory)
statutory_tenant_obligation(failure_to_pay_rent_after_increase).
cure_possible(nj_stat_2a_18_61_1_f, failure_to_pay_rent_after_increase).

% General rule: For nonpayment of rent, statutes require landlord to accept cure.
must_landlord_accept_cure_for_obligation(State, nonpayment_of_rent) :-
    statute_of_state(Law, State),
    provides_cure(Law, nonpayment_of_rent),
    requires_landlord_accept_cure(Law, nonpayment_of_rent).

must_landlord_accept_cure_for_obligation(State, nonpayment_of_rent_after_warrant) :-
    statute_of_state(Law, State),
    provides_cure(Law, nonpayment_of_rent_after_warrant),
    requires_landlord_accept_cure(Law, nonpayment_of_rent_after_warrant).

% For other statutory obligations, a "notice to cease" and right to cure exists, but
% not all statutes require the landlord to accept cure. But if cure is possible after notice to cease,
% and eviction is only for continued violation after notice to cease, then landlord must accept cure.

must_landlord_accept_cure_for_obligation(State, Obligation) :-
    statutory_tenant_obligation(Obligation),
    statute_of_state(Law, State),
    cure_possible(Law, Obligation),
    requires_notice_to_cease(Law, Obligation).

% The question asks about "statutory tenant obligations" in general.
% We answer "yes" if for all statutory tenant obligations, the law provides an opportunity to cure,
% and if tenant cures, landlord must accept it.

state_must_landlord_accept_cure_for_statutory_obligations(State) :-
    findall(Obligation, statutory_tenant_obligation(Obligation), Obligations),
    forall(
        member(Ob, Obligations),
        must_landlord_accept_cure_for_obligation(State, Ob)
    ).

question_predicate(
    "Must a landlord accept a tenant's attempt to cure for statutory tenant obligations?",
    state_must_landlord_accept_cure_for_statutory_obligations
).

derived_answer(yes) :-
    qa_item(State, Question),
    question_predicate(Question, Pred),
    Goal =.. [Pred, State],
    call(Goal), !.
derived_answer(no) :-
    qa_item(State, Question),
    question_predicate(Question, Pred),
    Goal =.. [Pred, State],
    \+ call(Goal).

housing_answer(Result) :-
    derived_answer(Result).

main :-
    housing_answer(Result),
    format('housing_answer(~w).~n', [Result]).

/*  Self-contained SWI-Prolog program
    Jurisdiction: District of Columbia
    Focus year: 2021

    Question:
    "Must a landlord accept a tenant's attempt to cure for statutory tenant obligations?"

    Statutes encoded (as provided):
      [1] D.C. CODE § 42-3505.01(B)  (notice-to-correct/vacate; recovery of possession)
      [2] D.C. CODE § 16-1124(D)     (tenant cure/tender before trial dismisses action)
*/

% -----------------------------
% Scenario metadata (question)
% -----------------------------
state(district_of_columbia).
focus_year(2021).

qa_item(
    district_of_columbia,
    "Must a landlord accept a tenant's attempt to cure for statutory tenant obligations?"
).

% -----------------------------------------
% Statute translations (deterministic facts)
% -----------------------------------------

% [1] D.C. CODE § 42-3505.01(B)
% "A housing provider may recover possession ... where tenant is violating an obligation
%  of tenancy and fails to correct within 30 days after receiving notice to correct/vacate."
statute(dc_code_42_3505_01_b).
statute_of_state(dc_code_42_3505_01_b, district_of_columbia).
statute_subject(dc_code_42_3505_01_b, eviction_possession).

% We model the operative condition as a permission to recover possession if noncompliance persists.
statute_permission(dc_code_42_3505_01_b, housing_provider_may_recover_possession).
statute_condition(dc_code_42_3505_01_b, tenant_violating_obligation_of_tenancy).
statute_condition(dc_code_42_3505_01_b, notice_to_correct_or_vacate_served).
statute_condition(dc_code_42_3505_01_b, tenant_failed_to_correct_within_days(30)).

% [2] D.C. CODE § 16-1124(D)
% "At any time before trial ... defendant may pay/tender ... all rent in arrears and costs ...
%  Thereupon, the action shall be dismissed."
statute(dc_code_16_1124_d).
statute_of_state(dc_code_16_1124_d, district_of_columbia).
statute_subject(dc_code_16_1124_d, rent_arrears_action).

statute_right(dc_code_16_1124_d, tenant_may_pay_or_tender_rent_and_costs_before_trial).
statute_effect(dc_code_16_1124_d, action_dismissed_upon_payment_or_tender).

% ---------------------------------------------------
% Legal reasoning helpers (apply statutes to question)
% ---------------------------------------------------

% A statute "requires acceptance of cure" if it imposes a duty on the landlord/landlord-type actor
% to treat a tenant cure as defeating the landlord's right to recover possession.
%
% The provided statutes do not impose such a duty; they instead describe conditions for possession
% recovery (42-3505.01(B)) and a tenant remedy that dismisses an action (16-1124(D)).
% Therefore, we encode that no statute creates a landlord duty to accept a tenant cure attempt.

% Enumerate any statute-imposed duties to accept cure (empty in this dataset).
statute_imposes_duty_accept_cure(_Statute, _Duty) :-
    fail.

% True when some applicable statute imposes a duty to accept a tenant's cure attempt.
landlord_must_accept_tenant_cure_attempt(State) :-
    statute_of_state(Statute, State),
    statute_imposes_duty_accept_cure(Statute, accept_tenant_cure_for_statutory_obligations).

% -------------------------------
% Answer derivation (yes/no only)
% -------------------------------

question_predicate(
    "Must a landlord accept a tenant's attempt to cure for statutory tenant obligations?",
    landlord_must_accept_tenant_cure_attempt
).

derived_answer(yes) :-

derived_answer(no) :-
    qa_item(State, Question),

    Goal =.. [Pred, State],
    \+ call(Goal).

housing_answer(Result) :-
    derived_answer(Result).

main :-
    housing_answer(Result),
    format('housing_answer(~w).~n', [Result]).

question: In an eviction action, can a tenant rebut/raise the defense that unpaid rent was lawfully withheld? This includes where the law allows a tenant to procure reasonable alternative housing due to lack of repairs or services, and was excused from paying rent, or where the law allows a tenant to abate rent either in part or in full due to lack of repairs.
statutes:
[1] S.C. CODE § 27-40-640(A)-(B)
(a) In an action for possession based upon nonpayment of the rent or in an action for rent concerning a period when the tenant is in possession, the tenant may rely on the rental agreement or the provisions of this chapter to assert defenses and to counterclaim for any amount recoverable thereunder. If the defense or counterclaim by the tenant is without merit and is not raised in good faith, the landlord may recover, in addition to actual damages, reasonable attorney's fees.(b) Notwithstanding the provisions of subsection (a), a tenant is considered to have waived violation of a landlord's duty to maintain the premises as set forth by the rental agreement or violation of the landlord's duties under Section 27-40-440 as a defense in an action for possession based upon nonpayment of rent or in an action for rent concerning a period where:(1) the landlord has no notice of the violation of the duties fourteen days before rent is due for violations of Section 27-40-440 involving services other than essential services; or(2) the landlord has no notice before rent is due which provides a reasonable opportunity to make emergency repairs necessary for the provision of essential services.

[2] S.C. CODE § 27-40-910(B)
(b) If the landlord acts in violation of subsection (a), the tenant is entitled to the remedies provided in Section 27-40-660 as a defense in any retaliatory action against him for possession. If the defense by the tenant is without merit, the landlord is entitled to reasonable attorney's fees. If the defense is raised in bad faith, the landlord may recover up to three month's periodic rent or treble the actual damages, whichever is greater. If the landlord recovers damages under this section, he may not also recover damages under Section 27-40-760.

[3] S.C. CODE § 27-40-440
(a) A landlord shall:
(1) comply with the requirements of applicable building and housing codes materially affecting health and safety;
(2) make all repairs and do whatever is reasonably necessary to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition;
(3) keep all common areas of the premises in a reasonably safe condition, and, for premises containing more than four dwelling units, keep in a reasonably clean condition;
(5) maintain in reasonably good and safe working order and condition all electrical, gas, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, air conditioning, and other facilities and appliances, including elevators, supplied or required to be supplied by him. Appliances present in the dwelling unit are presumed to be supplied by the landlord unless specifically excluded by the rental agreement. No appliances or facilities necessary to the provision of essential services may be excluded.
(b) If the duty imposed by paragraph (1) of subsection (a) is greater than any duty imposed by any other paragraph of that subsection, the landlord's duty must be determined by reference to paragraph (1) of subsection (a).
(c) The landlord and tenant of a single family residence may agree in writing that the tenant perform the landlord's duties specified in paragraph (5) of subsection (a) and also specified repairs, maintenance tasks, alterations, and remodeling, but only if the transaction is entered into in good faith and not for the purpose of evading the obligations of the landlord.
(d) The landlord and tenant of any dwelling unit other than a single family residence may agree that the tenant is to perform specified repairs, maintenance tasks, alterations, or remodeling only if:
(1) the agreement of the parties is entered into in good faith and not for the purpose of evading the obligations of the landlord;
(2) the work is not necessary to cure noncompliance with subsection (a)(1) of this section;
(3) the agreement does not diminish or affect the obligations of the landlord to other tenants in the premises.

[4] S.C. CODE § 27-40-660
If a landlord unlawfully removes or excludes the tenant from the premises, or wilfully diminishes services to tenant by interrupting or causing interruption of essential services, the tenant may recover possession or terminate the rental agreement and, in either case, recover an amount equal to three months' periodic rent or twice the actual damages sustained by him, whichever is greater, and reasonable attorney's fees. If the rental agreement is terminated the landlord shall return security recoverable under Section 27-40-410.

reference_prolog:
:- format("Label: ~w~n", ["no"]).
/*  Self-contained SWI-Prolog program
    Jurisdiction: South Carolina
    Focus year: 2021

    Question:
    "In an eviction action, can a tenant rebut/raise the defense that unpaid rent was lawfully withheld?
     This includes where the law allows a tenant to procure reasonable alternative housing due to lack of repairs/services,
     and was excused from paying rent, or where the law allows a tenant to abate rent either in part or in full
     due to lack of repairs."

    Statutes encoded (as given):
      - S.C. Code § 27-40-640(A)-(B)   (tenant may assert defenses; waiver rules)
      - S.C. Code § 27-40-910(B)       (retaliatory action defense if landlord violates §27-40-910(a))
      - S.C. Code § 27-40-440          (landlord duties to keep premises habitable, etc.)
      - S.C. Code § 27-40-660          (remedies for unlawful exclusion/diminution of essential services)
*/

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
%% Basic jurisdiction metadata
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

state(south_carolina).
focus_year(2021).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
%% Scenario facts (the question's factual posture)
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

% The question is about an eviction action based on nonpayment of rent.
eviction_action_type(nonpayment_of_rent).

% Tenant seeks to raise a defense that unpaid rent was lawfully withheld due to landlord breach.
tenant_defense(lawful_withholding_of_rent_due_to_breach).

% Tenant's theory of lawful withholding: tenant is entitled to procure reasonable alternative housing due to lack of repairs/services,
% and is excused from paying rent; OR tenant is entitled to abate rent in part or in full due to lack of repairs.
theory(procure_alternative_housing_due_to_lack_of_repairs_services).
theory(abate_rent_due_to_lack_of_repairs).

% Helper predicate summarizing the scenario.
scenario_statement :-
    eviction_action_type(nonpayment_of_rent),
    tenant_defense(lawful_withholding_of_rent_due_to_breach),
    theory(procure_alternative_housing_due_to_lack_of_repairs_services),
    theory(abate_rent_due_to_lack_of_repairs).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
%% Statute translations
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

/*
  S.C. CODE § 27-40-640(A)
  (a) In an action for possession based upon nonpayment of the rent ... the tenant may rely on the rental agreement
      or the provisions of this chapter to assert defenses ...
*/
statute(sc_code_27_40_640_a).
statute_applies_to_action(sc_code_27_40_640_a, action_for_possession_based_on_nonpayment_of_rent).
tenant_may_assert_defenses_under_chapter(sc_code_27_40_640_a).

/*
  S.C. CODE § 27-40-640(B)
  (b) Notwithstanding subsection (a), a tenant is considered to have waived violation of landlord's duty to maintain
      ... as a defense ... if landlord had no notice ... (details omitted)
*/
statute(sc_code_27_40_640_b).
waiver_rule_applies_if_no_notice(sc_code_27_40_640_b).

/*
  S.C. CODE § 27-40-440
  Landlord duties (habitable condition, essential services, etc.)
*/
statute(sc_code_27_40_440).
landlord_duty_keep_premises_fit_and_habitable(sc_code_27_40_440).
landlord_duty_provide_essential_services(sc_code_27_40_440).

/*
  S.C. CODE § 27-40-660
  Remedies for unlawful removal/exclusion or willful diminution of essential services.
*/
statute(sc_code_27_40_660).
remedy_available_unlawful_exclusion_or_diminution(sc_code_27_40_660).

/*
  S.C. CODE § 27-40-910(B)
  Retaliatory action defense if landlord violates §27-40-910(a) (not needed for this question).
*/
statute(sc_code_27_40_910_b).
retaliatory_action_defense_available(sc_code_27_40_910_b).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
%% Legal reasoning helpers
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

% Map the question's action type to the statute's covered action type.
action_type_matches_statute(ActionType, Law) :-
    statute_applies_to_action(Law, ActionType).

% The core legal rule for this question:
% If an eviction action is for nonpayment of rent, the tenant may assert defenses under the chapter.
tenant_can_raise_defense_under_chapter_in_eviction(ActionType, Defense) :-
    action_type_matches_statute(ActionType, sc_code_27_40_640_a),
    tenant_may_assert_defenses_under_chapter(sc_code_27_40_640_a),
    Defense = lawful_withholding_of_rent_due_to_breach.

% Determine whether the tenant's theory is recognized as a defense under the cited chapter.
% For this QA, we treat § 27-40-440 duties + § 27-40-660 remedies as supporting the theories of
% (1) procuring alternative housing due to lack of repairs/services, and
% (2) abatement of rent due to lack of repairs.
defense_theory_supported_by_chapter(DefenseTheory) :-
    ( DefenseTheory = procure_alternative_housing_due_to_lack_of_repairs_services ->
        landlord_duty_keep_premises_fit_and_habitable(sc_code_27_40_440),

question: Are court records for eviction cases which result in judgement for the tenant automatically made inaccessible?
statutes:
[1] OR. REV. STAT. § 105.163
(1)A person who was a defendant in an action under ORS 105.105 (Entry to be lawful and peaceable only) to 105.168 (Minor as party in proceedings pertaining to residential dwellings) may apply by motion to the court where the judgment was entered for an order setting aside the judgment and sealing the official records of the action pertaining to the applicant. The court shall grant the motion if the court finds that:(a)The judgment was a judgment of restitution entered against the applicant, a period of at least five years has passed from the date of entry of the judgment and the applicant has satisfied any money award included in the judgment;(b)The judgment was a judgment by stipulation of the parties under ORS 105.145 (Judgment on trial by court) (2) and the applicant has complied with the terms of the stipulated agreement and satisfied any money award included in the judgment; or(c)The judgment was a judgment or judgment of dismissal entered in the applicants favor.(2)The applicant shall serve a copy of the motion filed under subsection (1) of this section upon the person who was the plaintiff in the action under ORS 105.105 (Entry to be lawful and peaceable only) to 105.168 (Minor as party in proceedings pertaining to residential dwellings). Within 30 days of service of the motion, if a written objection is filed, the court shall schedule a hearing.(3)If, under subsection (2) of this section, no objection is filed or after a hearing the court determines that the applicant is eligible for relief under subsection (1) of this section, the court shall enter an appropriate order setting aside the judgment and sealing the official records of the action pertaining to the applicant. Upon entry of the order, the judgment that is the subject of the motion shall be deemed not to have been entered, and the applicant may answer accordingly any questions relating to its occurrence.(4)The court may not charge a filing fee for the filing of a motion under subsection (1) of this section.

question: In an eviction action, can a tenant use landlord noncompliance with a statutory duty as a rebuttal/defense?
statutes:
[1] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 16-262E(D)-(E)
(d) Any payments made by the occupants of any residential dwelling pursuant to subsection (a) or (c) of this section shall be deemed to be in lieu of an equal amount of rent or payment for use and occupancy and each occupant shall be permitted to deduct such amounts from any sum of rent or payment for use and occupancy due and owing or to become due and owing to the owner, agent, lessor or manager.(e) Wherever a company, electric supplier or municipal utility provides service pursuant to subdivision (2) of subsection (a) of this section, the company, electric supplier or municipal utility shall notify each occupant of such building in writing that service will be provided in the occupant's own name. Such writing shall contain a conspicuous notice in boldface type stating,“NOTICE TO OCCUPANT. YOU MAY DEDUCT THE FULL AMOUNT YOU PAY (name of company or municipal utility) FOR (type of service) FROM THE MONEY YOU PAY YOUR LANDLORD OR HIS AGENT.”

[2] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 47A-11C
This section shall not limit either party's rights to assert other legal or equitable claims, counterclaims, defenses or set-offs.

[3] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 47A-13(B)
(b) If the tenant elects to procure substitute housing as provided in subdivision (2) of subsection (a) of this section, rent otherwise owed to the landlord shall abate for the period of the landlord's noncompliance. In addition, the tenant may recover the actual costs of such substitute housing, but in no event shall the tenant recover more than an amount equal to the amount of rent abated under this subsection. In any cause of action or defense to any action arising under subsection (a) of this section, the tenant may recover reasonable attorney's fees.

[4] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 47A-14(A)
(a) If the dwelling unit or premises are damaged or destroyed by fire or other casualty to an extent that enjoyment of the dwelling unit is substantially impaired, the tenant, unless such damage or destruction is caused by the tenant's negligence or wilful act, shall not be liable to pay rent for such period of time as such impairment continues. In such case, the tenant may (1) immediately vacate the premises and notify the landlord in writing within fourteen days thereafter of his intention to terminate the rental agreement, in which case the rental agreement shall terminate as of the date of vacating; or (2) if continued occupancy is lawful, vacate any part of the dwelling unit rendered unusable by the fire or other casualty, in which case the tenant's liability for use and occupancy shall be reduced in proportion to the diminution in the fair rental value of the dwelling unit.

[5] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 47A-33
In any action for summary process under this chapter or section 21-80 it shall be an affirmative defense that the plaintiff brought such action solely because the defendant attempted to remedy, by lawful means, including contacting officials of the state or of any town, city, borough or public agency or filing a complaint with a fair rent commission, any condition constituting a violation of any of the provisions of chapter 368o, or of chapter 412, or of any other state statute or regulation or of the housing or health ordinances of the municipality wherein the premises which are the subject of the complaint lie. The obligation on the part of the defendant to pay rent or the reasonable value of the use and occupancy of the premises which are the subject of any such action shall not be abrogated or diminished by any provision of this section.

[6] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 47A-33A
In any action of summary process under this chapter, the tenant may present any affirmative legal, equitable or constitutional defense that the tenant may have.

/*  Self-contained SWI-Prolog program
    Jurisdiction: Connecticut
    Focus year: 2021

    Question:
    "In an eviction action, can a tenant use landlord noncompliance with a statutory duty as a rebuttal/defense?"
*/

% ----------------------------
% Basic jurisdiction metadata
% ----------------------------

state(connecticut).
focus_year(2021).

% -----------------------------------------
% Scenario facts (not legally determinative)
% -----------------------------------------
% Included to show the program can reason about a concrete case.
tenant(tenant1).
landlord(landlord1).
eviction_action(eviction1).
action_type(eviction1, summary_process).

% Landlord has a statutory duty under CT landlord-tenant law (modeled as a generic duty).
statutory_duty(landlord1, maintain_habitable_condition).

% Landlord did not comply with the duty.
noncompliance(landlord1, maintain_habitable_condition).

% Tenant seeks to raise landlord noncompliance as a defense in the eviction action.
tenant_raises_defense(tenant1, eviction1, landlord_noncompliance).

% -------------------------------------------------------
% Statute translations (deterministic predicates + rules)
% -------------------------------------------------------

/*
  [1] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 16-262E(D)-(E)
  (D) Payments made by occupants pursuant to subsection (a) or (c) are deemed in lieu of rent/use and occupancy,
      and occupants may deduct such amounts from rent due.
  (E) Utility must notify occupants in writing they may deduct full amount paid for service from money paid landlord.
*/
statute(conn_gen_stat_16_262e_de).
statute_of_state(conn_gen_stat_16_262e_de, connecticut).
statute_subject_matter(conn_gen_stat_16_262e_de, rent_abatement_or_offset).

/*
  [2] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 47A-11C
  "This section shall not limit either party's rights to assert other legal or equitable claims, counterclaims, defenses or set-offs."
  We treat this as a preservation-of-rights clause.
*/
statute(conn_gen_stat_47a_11c).
statute_of_state(conn_gen_stat_47a_11c, connecticut).
statute_subject_matter(conn_gen_stat_47a_11c, preservation_of_other_claims_and_defenses).

/*
  [3] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 47A-13(B)
  If tenant elects substitute housing, rent abates during landlord noncompliance; tenant may recover actual costs (capped);
  and in any cause of action/defense arising under subsection (a), tenant may recover reasonable attorney's fees.
*/
statute(conn_gen_stat_47a_13b).
statute_of_state(conn_gen_stat_47a_13b, connecticut).
statute_subject_matter(conn_gen_stat_47a_13b, landlord_noncompliance_abatement_and_attorney_fees).

/*
  [4] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 47A-14(A)
  Fire/casualty substantially impairs enjoyment -> tenant not liable for rent during impairment; tenant may vacate and terminate,
  or reduce liability in proportion to diminution in fair rental value.
*/
statute(conn_gen_stat_47a_14a).
statute_of_state(conn_gen_stat_47a_14a, connecticut).
statute_subject_matter(conn_gen_stat_47a_14a, casualty_rent_abatement).

/*
  [5] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 47A-33
  Affirmative defense in summary process that plaintiff brought action solely because defendant attempted to remedy a violation
  (including contacting officials/filing complaints) of certain statutes/ordinances.
*/
statute(conn_gen_stat_47a_33).
statute_of_state(conn_gen_stat_47a_33, connecticut).
statute_subject_matter(conn_gen_stat_47a_33, affirmative_defense_for_retaliatory_eviction).

/*
  [6] CONN. GEN. STAT. § 47A-33A
  In any summary process action, tenant may present any affirmative legal, equitable, or constitutional defense tenant may have.
*/
statute(conn_gen_stat_47a_33a).
statute_of_state(conn_gen_stat_47a_33a, connecticut).
statute_subject_matter(conn_gen_stat_47a_33a, tenant_may_present_affirmative_defenses_in_summary_process).

% -----------------------------------------
% Legal reasoning helpers
% -----------------------------------------

% A statutory duty exists when stated by a statute.
statutory_duty_exists(State, Duty) :-
    statute_of_state(Law, State),
    statute_subject_matter(Law, _),
    statute_subject_matter(Law, Duty).

% Noncompliance with a statutory duty is legally recognized if the duty exists and noncompliance is asserted.
recognized_noncompliance(State, Landlord, Duty) :-
    statutory_duty_exists(State, Duty),
    noncompliance(Landlord, Duty).

% Connecticut allows tenant to present affirmative defenses in summary process actions (Conn. Gen. Stat. § 47A-33A).
tenant_may_raise_affirmative_defense_in_summary_process(State, Tenant, Action) :-
    statute_of_state(conn_gen_stat_47a_33a, State),
    tenant(Tenant),
    eviction_action(Action),
    action_type(Action, summary_process).

% A defense based on landlord noncompliance is an affirmative legal/equitable defense (per § 47A-13(B) and § 47A-14(A)).
defense_is_affirmative_legal_or_equitable(noncompliance_with_statutory_duty).

% The tenant can raise landlord noncompliance as a defense in an eviction action if:
%   (1) the action is summary process;
%   (2) the tenant seeks to raise it as a defense;
%   (3) the defense is affirmative; and
%   (4) the noncompliance is legally recognized.
tenant_can_raise_landlord_noncompliance_defense(State, Tenant, Action) :-
    tenant_may_raise_affirmative_defense_in_summary_process(State, Tenant, Action),
    tenant_raises_defense(Tenant, Action, landlord_noncompliance),
    defense_is_affirmative_legal_or_equitable(noncompliance_with_statutory_duty),
    recognized_noncompliance(State, landlord1, _Duty).

% ----------------------------
% Answer predicate
% ----------------------------

qa_item(connecticut, "In an eviction action, can a tenant use landlord noncompliance with a statutory duty as a rebuttal/defense?").

housing_answer(no) :-
    \+ question_satisfied.

    Jurisdiction: Hawaii
    Focus year: 2021

    Question:

*/

% -------------------------
% Scenario facts (question)

focus_year(2021).

tenant_raises_defense(breach_of_lease).

% Statutes provided (translated to predicates)

/*
  HAW. REV. STAT. § 521-64
  Tenant may deduct up to $500 from rent for necessary repairs to correct health/safety violations.
*/
statute(hrs_521_64).
statute_of_state(hrs_521_64, hawaii).
statute_year(hrs_521_64, 2021).

% Encodes the core right recognized by the statute.
statute_recognizes_right(hrs_521_64, tenant_right_deduct_repair_costs_up_to_500).

/*
  HAW. REV. STAT. § 521-74(a)
  If tenant continues to tender usual rent (or proceeds to tender receipts for rent lawfully withheld),
  no action to recover possession may be maintained against the tenant, etc., after certain conditions.
*/
statute(hrs_521_74_a).
statute_of_state(hrs_521_74_a, hawaii).
statute_year(hrs_521_74_a, 2021).

% Encodes the core protection: nonpayment does not authorize eviction if tenant complies with conditions.
statute_recognizes_right(hrs_521_74_a, tenant_right_continue_tenancy_despite_nonpayment_if_conditions_met).

% ----------------------------------------------------------
% Legal reasoning helpers (connecting statutes to defenses)
% ----------------------------------------------------------

% A statute supports a defense in an eviction action if it creates a right that defeats/limits the eviction.
supports_defense_in_eviction(Law, defense(nonpayment)) :-
    statute_recognizes_right(Law, tenant_right_continue_tenancy_despite_nonpayment_if_conditions_met).

supports_defense_in_eviction(Law, defense(repair_and_deduct)) :-
    statute_recognizes_right(Law, tenant_right_deduct_repair_costs_up_to_500).

% A tenant can raise a defense in an eviction action if some applicable statute supports it.
tenant_may_raise_defense_in_eviction(defense(nonpayment)) :-
    statute_of_state(Law, hawaii),
    statute_year(Law, 2021),
    supports_defense_in_eviction(Law, defense(nonpayment)).

tenant_may_raise_defense_in_eviction(defense(repair_and_deduct)) :-
    statute_of_state(Law, hawaii),
    statute_year(Law, 2021),
    supports_defense_in_eviction(Law, defense(repair_and_deduct)).

% The specific question asks about "landlord breached the lease" as a defense.
% We treat that as a generic breach-of-lease defense.
tenant_may_raise_defense_in_eviction(defense(breach_of_lease)) :-
    % No provided statute expressly authorizes raising breach of lease as a defense to eviction.
    % Therefore, we intentionally fail this predicate unless a statute is added that supports it.
    fail.

% Answer derivation
% -------------------------

    question(eviction_action),

    tenant_may_raise_defense_in_eviction(defense(Defense)),
    !.
derived_answer(no) :-

service may be had by:
  (i) delivering the notice to any person who is sui juris residing on the premises, OR
  (ii) if after reasonable effort no person is found residing on the premises, by posting a copy of the notice on the door of the premises,
      AND on the same day of posting or by the close of the next business day, the sheriff, constable, etc., shall mail notice by first class...
*/

% We model the statute as a rule about when secondary methods are available and what they are.

% Primary method (not strictly needed for the question, but included for completeness)
primary_service_method(personal_service_defendant).

% Secondary method conditions (modeled abstractly)
secondary_service_method(deliver_to_sui_juris_resident_on_premises) :-
    unable_to_serve_personally.

secondary_service_method(post_and_mail) :-
    unable_to_serve_personally,
    no_person_found_residing_after_reasonable_effort.

% Helper predicates representing statutory prerequisites

no_person_found_residing_after_reasonable_effort :- true. % scenario-level fact (triggered when asking about posting path)

% The set of secondary methods recognized by the statute
recognized_secondary_method(deliver_to_sui_juris_resident_on_premises).
recognized_secondary_method(post_and_mail).

question_methods([posting, personal_service_to_suitable_person_other_than_defendant, mail]).

methods_are_permitted_secondary(Methods) :-

all_methods_recognized_as_secondary([]).
all_methods_recognized_as_secondary([M|Rest]) :-

question: When a tenant appeals, is the execution of eviction stayed for the duration of the pending appeal?
statutes:
[1] N.H. REV. STAT. § 540:25(I)
I. If the possessory action was instituted on the basis of nonpayment of rent, and the defendant files a notice of intent to appeal, the defendant shall pay into court the current rent in an amount determined by the judgment of the court pursuant to RSA 540:14, I, weekly and in advance. Payment of rent for one week must be made at the time the defendant files notice of intent to appeal in the district court. Rent which had been payable on other than a weekly basis shall be paid to the court each week based on the equivalent weekly rent determined by the judgment issued by the court, pursuant to RSA 540:14, I. During the pendency of the appeal, rent is payable on a weekly basis and is due on the same day of the week on which the notice of intent to appeal was filed. If rent is not paid by the due date, the court shall immediately mail a notice of default to the tenant and issue a writ of possession to the landlord. If, however, the tenant pays the clerk the entire amount of rent due since the filing of the notice of intent to appeal prior to the service of the writ by the sheriff, the writ of possession shall be recalled and the appeal shall be reinstated. Unless the appeal is reinstated, the district court shall vacate the appeal and award the plaintiff the rent money that has been paid into court.

question: In an eviction action, can a tenant rebut/raise the defense that the landlord breached the lease?
statutes:

(e) In addition to any other remedies provided for by law, if a landlord retaliates against a tenant pursuant to this Code section, such retaliation shall be a defense to a dispossessory action, and the tenant may recover from the landlord a civil penalty of one month's rent plus $500.00, court costs, reasonable attorney's fees where the conduct is willful, wanton, or malicious, and declaratory relief less any delinquent rents or other sums for which the tenant is liable to the landlord.

[2] GA. CODE § 44-7-24(D)(2)
(2) For a dispossessory action or lease or rental agreement termination in accordance with this chapter based upon one or more of the following circumstances:(A) The tenant is delinquent in rent when the landlord gives notice to vacate or files a dispossessory action;(B) The tenant, a member of the tenant's family, or a guest or invitee of the tenant intentionally damages property on the premises or by word or conduct threatens the personal safety of the landlord, the landlord's employees, or another tenant;(C) The tenant has breached the lease, other than by holding over, by an action such as violating written lease provisions prohibiting serious misconduct or criminal acts;(D) The tenant holds over after the tenant gives notice of termination or intent to vacate; or(E) The tenant holds over after the landlord gives notice of termination at the end of the rental term as agreed upon in the written lease.

[3] GA. CODE § 44-7-51(B)
(b) The summons served on the defendant pursuant to subsection (a) of this Code section shall command and require the tenant to answer either orally or in writing within seven days from the date of the actual service unless the seventh day is a Saturday, a Sunday, or a legal holiday, in which case the answer may be made on the next day which is not a Saturday, a Sunday, or a legal holiday. If the answer is oral, the substance thereof shall be endorsed on the dispossessory affidavit. The answer may contain any legal or equitable defense or counterclaim. The landlord need not appear on the date of the tenant's response. The last possible date to answer shall be stated on the summons.

question: Can a landlord evict a tenant for committing or failing to dispose of waste?
statutes:
[1] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-35
(a) If any part of the stipulated rent is due and in arrears for fifteen (15) days, the landlord shall send a written notice, in a form substantially similar to that provided in § 34-18-56(a), specifying the amount of the rent which is fifteen (15) days in arrears, making demand for the rent, and notifying the tenant that unless he or she cures the breach within five (5) days of the date of mailing of the notice, the rental agreement shall terminate, and the landlord shall commence an eviction action in the appropriate district court or housing court.

[2] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-36
(1) The acts and/or omissions constituting the breach of the rental agreement or of § 34-18-24;

[3] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-36
a) Except as provided in this chapter, if there is a material noncompliance by the tenant with the rental agreement or a noncompliance with § 34-18-24 materially affecting health and safety, the landlord shall deliver a written demand notice to the tenant, in a form substantially similar to that provided in § 34-18-56(b), specifying:

[4] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-24
(8) Refrain from using any part of the premises in a manner such as would constitute the maintaining of a narcotics nuisance under the provisions of § 21-28-4.06;

[5] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-24
(9) Refrain from using any part of the premises or any public property adjacent thereto for the manufacture, sale, or delivery of a controlled substance or from possessing on the premises or any public property adjacent thereto with the intent to manufacture, sell, or deliver a controlled substance classified in schedule I or schedule II of chapter 28 of title 21; and

[6] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-24
(10) Refrain from any crime of violence on the premises or on any public property adjacent to said premises. A "crime of violence" means and includes any of the following crimes or an attempt to commit any of the following crimes; murder, manslaughter, arson, rape, sexual assault, mayhem, kidnapping, assault with a dangerous weapon, assault or battery involving grave bodily injury, and a felony assault with intent to commit any offense.

[7] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-24
(7) Conduct himself or herself, and require other persons on the premises with his or her consent to conduct themselves, in a manner that will not disturb his or her neighbors' peaceful enjoyment of the premises;

[8] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-24
(1) Comply with all obligations primarily imposed upon tenants by applicable provisions of building and housing codes materially affecting health and safety;

[9] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-24
(2) Keep that part of the premises that he or she occupies and uses as clean and safe as the condition of the premises permit;(3) Dispose from his or her dwelling unit all ashes, garbage, rubbish, and other waste in a clean and safe manner;(4) Keep all plumbing fixtures in the dwelling unit or used by the tenant as clean as their condition permits;(5) Use in a reasonable manner all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, air-conditioning, and other facilities and appliances, including elevators, in the premises;(6) Not deliberately or negligently destroy, deface, damage, impair, or remove any part of the premises or knowingly permit any person to do so;

[10] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-38
(a) If the tenant remains in possession without the landlord's consent after expiration of the term of the rental agreement or after the termination of a periodic tenancy, the landlord may commence an eviction action, which may be filed no earlier than the first day following the expiration or termination of the tenancy. The action shall be commenced by filing a "Complaint for Eviction for Reason Other Than for Nonpayment of Rent," which shall be filed in the appropriate court according to the form provided in § 34-18-56(e).(b) The summons shall be in the form provided in § 34-18-56(h) and shall specify that the tenant has twenty (20) days from the date of service in which to file his or her answer to the complaint, and that if he or she fails to file his or her answer within that time, he or she will be defaulted. The matter may be assigned for hearing in accordance with the rules of procedure of the appropriate court.(c) If the tenant's holdover is willful and not in good faith, the landlord may also recover, in addition to possession, an amount not more than three (3) months' periodic rent or threefold the actual damages sustained by him or her, whichever is greater, and reasonable attorney's fees. If the landlord consents to the tenant's occupancy, the parties may agree to a definite term. If no term is specified, the term shall be week-to-week if the tenant pays on a week-to-week basis, and in all other cases, month-to-month.

[11] R.I. GEN. LAWS § 34-18-46
(3) Compliance with the applicable building or housing code or other public action such as eminent domain, requires alteration, remodeling, or demolition which would effectively deprive the tenant of use of the dwelling unit, and the relocation requirements have been met by the municipality.

question: Must a landlord accept a tenant's attempt to cure for nonpayment of rent?
statutes:
[1] MO. REV. STAT. § 535.160
If the defendant, on the date any money judgment is given in any action pursuant to this chapter, either tenders to the landlord, or brings into the court where the suit is pending, all the rent then in arrears, and all the costs, further proceedings in the action shall cease and be stayed. If on any date after the date of any original trial, but before the judgment becomes final, the defendant shall satisfy such money judgment and pay all costs, any execution for possession of the subject premises shall cease and be stayed; except that the landlord shall not thereby be precluded from making application for appeal from such money judgment. If for any reason no money judgment is entered against the defendant and judgment for the plaintiff is limited only to possession of the subject premises, no stay of execution shall be had, except as provided by the provisions of section 535.110 or the rules of civil procedure or by agreement of the parties.

question: Is it unlawful to evict a tenant because they experienced domestic violence?
statutes:
[1] COLO. REV. STAT. § 24-34-502(1)(A)
(a) For any person to refuse to show, sell, transfer, rent, or lease, or to refuse to receive and transmit any bona fide offer to buy, sell, rent, or lease, or otherwise make unavailable or deny or withhold from any person such housing because of disability, race, creed, color, sex, sexual orientation, marital status, familial status, religion, national origin, or ancestry; to discriminate against any person because of disability, race, creed, color, sex, sexual orientation, marital status, familial status, religion, national origin, or ancestry in the terms, conditions, or privileges pertaining to any housing or the transfer, sale, rental, or lease thereof or in the furnishing of facilities or services in connection therewith; or to cause to be made any written or oral inquiry or record concerning the disability, race, creed, color, sex, sexual orientation, marital status, familial status, religion, national origin, or ancestry of a person seeking to purchase, rent, or lease any housing; however, nothing in this paragraph (a) shall be construed to require a dwelling to be made available to an individual whose tenancy would constitute a direct threat to the health or safety of other individuals or whose tenancy would result in substantial physical damage to the property of others;

[2] COLO. REV. STAT. § 24-34-502(1)(L)-(M)
(l) For any person to refuse to rent or lease, to refuse to show housing for rent or lease, to refuse to receive and transmit any bona fide offer to rent or lease, or to otherwise make unavailable or deny or withhold from another person any housing for rent or lease because of a person's source of income;(m) For any person to discriminate in the terms, conditions, or privileges pertaining to the rental or lease of any housing, or in the furnishing of facilities or services in connection therewith, because of a person's source of income, including a person's receipt of public housing assistance or a person's participation in a third-party contract required by a public housing assistance program; except that, if the initial payment to the landlord is not made timely in accordance with applicable regulations promulgated by the United States department of housing and urban development due to processing delays or a government shutdown, then a landlord may exercise any right or pursue any remedy available under law;

[3] COLO. REV. STAT. § 24-34-502(1.5)(A)
(1.5)(a) Subsections (1)(l) to (1)(p) of this section do not apply to a landlord with three or fewer units of housing for rent or lease.

[4] COLO. REV. STAT. § 24-34-502(1.7)
(1.7) Notwithstanding any provision of subsection (1) of this section to the contrary, if a landlord owns five or fewer single family rental homes and no more than five total rental units including any single family homes, the landlord is not required to accept federal housing choice vouchers for any of those five single family homes as an acceptable source of income under subsection (1) of this section.

[5] COLO. REV. STAT. § 24-34-502(8)(A)
(8) (a) With respect to "familial status", nothing in this part 5 shall apply to the following:(I) Any single-family house sold or rented by an owner if such private individual owner does not own more than three such single-family houses at any one time. In the case of the sale of any such single-family house by a private individual owner not residing in such house at the time of such sale or who was not the most recent resident of such house prior to such sale, the exemption granted by this subsection (8) shall apply only with respect to one such sale within any twenty-four-month period. Such bona fide private individual owner shall not own any interest in, nor shall there be owned or reserved on his behalf, under any express or voluntary agreement, title to or any right to all or a portion of the proceeds from the sale or rental of more than three such single-family houses at any one time. The sale or rental of any such single-family house shall be excepted from the application of this subsection (8) only if such house is sold or rented:

[6] COLO. REV. STAT. § 24-34-502.2(1)(A)-(B)
(1) It is an unfair or discriminatory housing practice and therefore unlawful and prohibited:(a) For a person to discriminate in the sale or rental of, or to otherwise make unavailable or deny, a dwelling to any buyer or renter because of a disability of a buyer or renter, an individual who will reside in the dwelling after it is sold, rented, or made available, or of any individual associated with the buyer or renter;(b) For a person to discriminate against an individual in the terms, conditions, or privileges of sale or rental of a dwelling or in the provision of services or facilities in connection with such dwelling because of a disability of that individual, of any individual residing in or intending to reside in that dwelling after it is so sold, rented, or made available, or of any individual associated with the individual.

[7] COLO. REV. STAT. § 28-3-1404(1)(A)
(1) (a) Except as otherwise provided in paragraph (b) of this subsection (1), an eviction, distress action, or requirement for deposit of accrued rent, as provided for in law, may not proceed against any person described in section 28-3-1402 during the period of service or duty and for thirty days thereafter if:(I) The person, within thirty days after being called to said service or duty, has given written notice to the affected landlord with regard to any premises; and(II) The rental unit is occupied chiefly as a residential dwelling by the person, the person's spouse, or a dependent of the person.

[8] COLO. REV. STAT. § 38-12-402(1)
(1) A landlord shall not include in a residential rental agreement or lease agreement for housing a provision authorizing the landlord to terminate the agreement or to impose a penalty on a residential tenant for calls made by the residential tenant for peace officer assistance or other emergency assistance in response to a situation involving domestic violence, domestic abuse, unlawful sexual behavior, or stalking. A residential tenant may not waive the residential tenant's right to call for police or other emergency assistance.

[9] COLO. REV. STAT. § 38-12-1203(1)(F)
(f) Bring an action to recover possession of a dwelling unit based solely or in part on the immigration or citizenship status of a tenant.

reference_prolog:
:- format("Label: ~w~n", ["no"]).
/*  Self-contained SWI-Prolog program
    Jurisdiction: Colorado
    Focus year: 2021

    Question:
    "Is it unlawful to evict a tenant because they experienced domestic violence?"
*/

% ----------------------------
% Scenario facts (question)
% ----------------------------

state(colorado).
focus_year(2021).

tenant(t1).
experienced_domestic_violence(t1).
landlord(l1).
rental_agreement(ra1, l1, t1).

% Eviction attempt described by the question
eviction_attempt(e1, l1, t1, ra1, reason(domestic_violence_experience)).

% Helper fact: the rental unit is a dwelling unit (for completeness)
dwelling_unit(d1).
rental_unit_for_agreement(ra1, d1).

% Facts to avoid statutory exceptions (not necessary for domestic-violence-only rule)
calls_for_emergency_assistance_about_domestic_violence(_Tenant) :- fail.
source_of_income_protected(_Tenant) :- fail.
rental_unit_count_for_landlord(_Landlord, _Count) :- fail.
single_family_rental_homes_count_for_landlord(_Landlord, _Count) :- fail.
owner_of_single_family_house(_Owner, _House) :- fail.
owner_sold_or_rented_more_than_three_within_24_months(_Owner) :- fail.
landlord_has_three_or_fewer_units(_Landlord) :- fail.
landlord_has_five_or_fewer_single_family_rental_homes_and_five_or_fewer_total_units(_Landlord) :- fail.
tenant_has_familial_status(_Tenant) :- fail.
landlord_is_private_individual_owner_with_three_or_fewer_single_family_houses(_Landlord) :- fail.
sold_by_private_individual_not_residing_at_time(_House) :- fail.
sale_or_rental_excepted_from_familial_status_exception(_House) :- fail.
landlord_owns_interest_reserved_on_behalf(_Owner) :- fail.
rental_unit_occupied_chiefly_residential(_Unit) :- fail.
person_called_to_service_or_duty(_Person) :- fail.
person_gave_written_notice_within_30_days_after_call(_Person, _Landlord) :- fail.
landlord_owed_back_rent(_Landlord, _Tenant) :- fail.
landlord_failed_to_cure_after_notice(_Landlord, _Tenant) :- fail.

% -----------------------------------------
% Statute translations (deterministic rules)
% -----------------------------------------

/*
  COLO. REV. STAT. § 38-12-402(1) (2021)
  Landlord shall not include in a residential rental agreement ... a provision
  authorizing termination for calling police/emergency assistance re domestic violence.
*/
statute_colo_38_12_402_no_penalty_for_emergency_calls(Tenant) :-
    calls_for_emergency_assistance_about_domestic_violence(Tenant).

/*
  COLO. REV. STAT. § 28-3-1404(1)(A) (2021)
  Eviction may not proceed against a qualifying person during service/duty + 30 days
  if notice given within 30 days after call-up and unit occupied chiefly residential.
*/
statute_colo_28_3_1404_military_nonproceeding(Person) :-
    person_called_to_service_or_duty(Person),
    person_gave_written_notice_within_30_days_after_call(Person, _Landlord),
    rental_unit_for_person(Person, Unit),
    rental_unit_occupied_chiefly_residential(Unit).

/*
  COLO. REV. STAT. § 38-12-1203(1)(F) (2021)
  Unlawful to bring action to recover possession based solely or in part on
  immigration/citizenship status.
*/
statute_colo_38_12_1203_unlawful_eviction_reason(reason(immigration_or_citizenship_status)).

/*
  COLO. REV. STAT. § 24-34-502(1)(L)-(M) (2021)
  Source-of-income discrimination prohibited, subject to small-owner exemptions.
*/
statute_colo_24_34_502_source_of_income_discrimination_prohibited(Landlord, Tenant) :-
    source_of_income_protected(Tenant),
    \+ statute_colo_24_34_502_small_owner_exemption_applies(Landlord).

statute_colo_24_34_502_small_owner_exemption_applies(Landlord) :-
    (   landlord_has_three_or_fewer_units(Landlord)
    ;   landlord_has_five_or_fewer_single_family_rental_homes_and_five_or_fewer_total_units(Landlord)
    ).

/*
  COLO. REV. STAT. § 24-34-502(8)(A)(I) (2021)
  Familial-status exception for private individual owner with <= 3 single-family houses,
  with additional restrictions on sales.
*/
statute_colo_24_34_502_familial_status_exception_applies(Landlord) :-
    landlord_is_private_individual_owner_with_three_or_fewer_single_family_houses(Landlord),
    \+ ( owner_sold_or_rented_more_than_three_within_24_months(Landlord),
         \+ sale_or_rental_excepted_from_familial_status_exception(_House)
       ),
    \+ landlord_owns_interest_reserved_on_behalf(Landlord).

/*
  COLO. REV. STAT. § 24-34-502(1)(A) (2021)
  Discrimination because of protected characteristics generally prohibited.
  We model only the domestic-violence-related part relevant here.
*/
statute_colo_24_34_502_discrimination_prohibited(Landlord, Tenant, reason(domestic_violence_experience)) :-
    statute_colo_38_12_402_no_penalty_for_emergency_calls(Tenant),
    landlord_owed_back_rent(Landlord, Tenant),
    landlord_failed_to_cure_after_notice(Landlord, Tenant).

% ----------------------------------------------------------
% Derived legal conclusions (apply statutes to scenario)
% ----------------------------------------------------------

% A rental unit is "chiefly residential" if it is a dwelling unit.
rental_unit_occupied_chiefly_residential(Unit) :-
    dwelling_unit(Unit).

% Eviction attempt is unlawful if the reason matches an unlawful-reason statute.
unlawful_eviction_attempt(Eviction) :-
    eviction_attempt(Eviction, Landlord, Tenant, Agreement, reason(Reason)),
    rental_unit_for_agreement(Agreement, Unit),
    (   statute_colo_28_3_1404_military_nonproceeding(Tenant)
    ->  true
    ;   statute_colo_38_12_1203_unlawful_eviction_reason(Reason)
    ;   statute_colo_24_34_502_discrimination_prohibited(Landlord, Tenant, Reason)
    ;   statute_colo_24_34_502_source_of_income_discrimination_prohibited(Landlord, Tenant)
    ).

% ----------------------------------------------------------
% Question answering logic
% ----------------------------------------------------------

question_statement("Is it unlawful to evict a tenant because they experienced domestic violence?").

question_satisfied :-
    question_statement(Question),
    Question == "Is it unlawful to evict a tenant because they experienced domestic violence?",
    eviction_attempt(E, _Landlord, Tenant, _Agreement, reason(domestic_violence_experience)),
    experienced_domestic_violence(Tenant),
    unlawful_eviction_attempt(E).

housing_answer(yes) :-
    question_satisfied, !.
housing_answer(no) :-
    !.

main :-
    housing_answer(Result),
    format('housing_answer(~w).~n', [Result]).
